signals: Treatment with one of four approved anti-influenza drugs may be considered if the patient presents within 48 hours of symptom onset
The benefit of treatment is greatest when antiviral therapy is started within 24 hours of symptom onset
These drugs decrease the duration of illness by about 24 hours
Laboratory testing may be useful in hospitalized patients with suspected influenza

extraction.summary: Influenza is an acute viral respiratory infection caused by influenza A, B, or C viruses that causes significant worldwide morbidity and mortality. The hallmark of infection is abrupt onset of fever, cough, chills or sweats, myalgias, and malaise, with most cases diagnosed clinically in outpatient settings. Treatment with antivirals within 48 hours (ideally 24 hours) of symptom onset can reduce illness duration by approximately 24 hours.

population.age_criteria: All ages affected; particularly severe in children < 5 years (especially < 2 years), adults ≥ 65 years
population.risk_criteria: High-risk groups include pregnant/postpartum women, immunosuppressed patients, chronic medical conditions (asthma, heart disease, diabetes, kidney disease), morbid obesity, American Indians/Alaska Natives, institutionalized adults
population.disease_definition: Acute viral respiratory infection with abrupt onset of fever, cough, chills/sweats, myalgias, and malaise
population.exclusions: None specified for diagnosis; contraindications exist for specific antiviral treatments
population.severity_staging: Uncomplicated vs severe illness; severe includes viral/bacterial pneumonia with respiratory and multiorgan failure

key_thresholds.context: Maximum time from symptom onset for antiviral treatment
Ideal time from symptom onset for maximum antiviral benefit
High risk for influenza (2 pts fever+cough, 2 pts myalgias, 1 pt chills/sweats, 1 pt onset <48hrs)
Maximum time from symptom onset for antiviral effectiveness
Within 72 hours in residents on same unit for long-term care facility

recommendations.recommendation: Annual influenza vaccination for all people 6 months and older without contraindications
Base antiviral treatment decision on clinical diagnosis, not test results; obtain lab diagnosis when results will change management
Severe illness or high-risk patients should receive antiviral treatment regardless of symptom duration
Consider antivirals within 48 hours (ideally 24 hours) of symptom onset to reduce symptom duration
Initiate antiviral treatment within 48 hours of symptom onset
recommendations.notes: Greatest benefit when started early; may still be beneficial beyond 48 hours in hospitalized patients

recommendations.recommendation: Do not use more than one antiviral agent or higher than recommended dosages
recommendations.notes: No demonstrated benefit to combination therapy

recommendations.recommendation: Corticosteroids not recommended unless patient has another approved indication
recommendations.notes: May be harmful in influenza treatment

recommendations.recommendation: Consider chemoprophylaxis only for specific high-risk populations

medications.dose: Adults: 75mg BID x 5 days; Children: weight-based dosing
medications.indication: Treatment and prevention of influenza A and B
Treatment and prevention of influenza A and B

medications.dose: Adults: 600mg IV single dose; Children 2-12yrs: 12mg/kg IV single dose
medications.indication: Treatment of uncomplicated influenza A and B

medications.dose: 40-80kg: 40mg PO single dose; ≥80kg: 80mg PO single dose
medications.indication: Treatment of uncomplicated influenza in patients ≥12 years

medications.dose: Adults/children ≥13 years: 600 mg single dose IV; Children 2-12 years: 12 mg/kg (max 600 mg) single dose IV
medications.indication: Treatment of uncomplicated acute influenza A and B

medications.dose: 40-79 kg: 40 mg single dose PO; ≥80 kg: 80 mg single dose PO
medications.indication: Treatment of uncomplicated acute influenza in patients ≥12 years

medications.dose: Standard dosing (referenced but specific doses not provided in excerpt)
medications.indication: First-line treatment for pregnant women with influenza

red_flags: Bronchospasm in patients with underlying lung disease
Development of lower respiratory tract disease while on antivirals
Severe hypersensitivity or anaphylaxis to prescribed antiviral
extraction.follow_up: Monitor for complications in high-risk patients; symptom resolution typically occurs in 2-8 days; patients remain infectious 1-2 days before symptom onset and 5-7 days afterward
extraction.escalation_path: Hospitalize patients with severe illness, respiratory failure, or complications; consider ICU for multiorgan failure or ARDS; specialist consultation for neurologic or cardiac complications; intravenous peramivir for severe disease if oral oseltamivir contraindicated
